title: Daily stock checks: what the law requires and how to make them efficient
subtitle: The legal inspection frequency for different species, and how to build a morning routine that covers all animals in minimum time.
excerpt: UK welfare codes set minimum inspection frequencies that vary by species and management system. A smallholder keeping sheep, pigs, and poultry has different legal requirements for each. Understanding those requirements, and building a daily walk that makes them practical, is both a compliance issue and the foundation of early disease detection.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Codes of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003), Sheep (2010), Cattle (2010), Poultry (2002), gov.uk. Animal Welfare Act 2006, legislation.gov.uk.

glossaryTerms.definition: The minimum frequency at which a keeper is expected to check their animals under UK welfare codes. Pigs: at least twice daily. Sheep: at least daily, more frequently around lambing. Poultry: at least once daily (more frequently in intensive systems). Rabbits and goats: at least daily under general animal welfare duty of care. These are minima, not targets; animals that are ill, pregnant, or young need more frequent checks.

content.text: The daily check is the single management action that prevents the most welfare problems on a smallholding. An animal found with a problem at the morning check has at most 24 hours of deterioration to address. An animal not found until the same problem has run for three days without observation has a significantly worse prognosis.

under UK welfare codes: pigs must be checked at least twice daily; sheep, cattle, and poultry at least once daily. For animals around parturition, the Sheep Welfare Code recommends checking pregnant ewes at least twice daily in the final two weeks before lambing, and more frequently in bad weather. For a small mixed holding, twice daily is a reasonable default across all species.

A practical morning check works from a distance first, then close up for any animals that need it. On arrival at the field or yard, pause and observe before opening any gate: count the animals and confirm all are standing or moving normally. Any animal not visible, not rising, or obviously lame is the first priority before you continue.

As you work through feeding, letting out, and water, watch each group as you go: note appetite, any animal apart from the rest, and any unusual posture. Do not rush past animals. The walk-through is the check.

For food-producing animals, a brief daily log of health observations supports the medicine records required by law. Write down the date, the animals checked, and any observations. A phone photo of an unusual symptom is often more useful than a written description when you call the vet.

The duty of care does not pause when you are away. Before any absence, arrange a competent person to perform the checks and leave a written brief: which animals are due attention, what normal looks like, and your vet's number. A person covering your stock who does not know what normal looks like cannot identify a problem effectively.
